lessons.title: One Big Tree of Folders
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On Windows you might keep files on the C: drive and a USB stick on D:. Linux does something simpler: there is just *one* big tree of folders, and everything lives somewhere inside it.

**Build.** That tree starts at a single folder called the **root**, written as a plain slash: `/`. ("Folder" and "directory" mean the same thing; Linux usually says *directory*.) Every other folder branches off from `/`. So `/home` is the folder named `home` that sits directly inside the root, and `/home/alice` is the folder `alice` inside `home`.

There are no drive letters. When you plug in a USB stick or add a second disk, Linux *mounts* it onto some folder in the tree, so its contents simply appear at a path like `/media/usb`. From your point of view it is all one tree.

Think of it like a building directory in a lobby: a single map that tells you which floor holds what, so you never wander. The root `/` is the lobby; the top-level folders are the floors.

```bash
ls /            # list the top-level folders inside the root
cd /home/alice  # move into alice's home folder
pwd             # "print working directory": shows where you are now
```

A path that starts with `/` is an **absolute path**: it spells out the route all the way from the root, so it means the same thing no matter where you currently are.

**Recap.** Linux organizes all files as one tree starting at `/` (the root). No drive letters; extra disks are mounted onto folders inside that one tree.

items.prompt: In Linux, what does a single forward slash `/` by itself refer to?
choices: The root: the single top folder that the whole tree starts from
The current folder you are in
items.explanation: `/` is the root, the one folder at the very top of the tree. Everything else lives somewhere inside it. The current folder is `.` and your home folder is written `~`.

items.prompt: How does Linux differ from Windows when it comes to disks like C: and D:?
choices: Linux uses drive letters too, just lowercase
Linux has no drive letters; every disk appears as a folder inside the one tree
Linux can only use one disk at a time
Linux hides all disks from the user
items.explanation: Linux has no drive letters. A second disk or USB stick is mounted onto a folder (a mount point), so its files just show up at a path like /media/usb inside the single tree.

items.prompt: What does the path `/home/alice` describe?
choices: A folder named alice, inside a folder named home, inside the root
A command that creates a user
items.explanation: Reading left to right from the root: `/` then `home` then `alice`. Each slash separates one level of folder from the next, so this is the alice folder inside home inside the root.

items.prompt: You plug in a USB stick and its files appear at `/media/usb`. What happened?
choices: The stick was given the drive letter U:
The stick was mounted onto the /media/usb folder so its contents appear there in the tree
Linux copied the files into your home folder
The stick replaced the root folder
items.explanation: Mounting attaches a disk's contents onto a folder in the existing tree. After mounting at /media/usb, browsing that folder shows the stick's files. There is still just one tree.

items.prompt: Why is a path that starts with `/`, such as `/etc/hosts`, called an absolute path?
choices: Because it can never be deleted
Because it spells out the full route from the root, so it means the same thing wherever you are
Because it is the fastest kind of path
Because only the root user can read it
items.explanation: An absolute path begins at the root `/` and lists every step from there, so it is unambiguous no matter your current folder. A path that does NOT start with `/` is relative to wherever you currently are.

items.prompt: Which command tells you which folder you are currently sitting in?
items.explanation: `pwd` means "print working directory" and prints your current location as an absolute path. `ls` lists contents, `cd` moves you, and `mkdir` makes a new folder.

items.prompt: The lesson compares the filesystem to a building directory in a lobby. What is that analogy getting at?
choices: Files are heavy like a building
There is one shared map starting from a single point, so you always know where to look
Linux is only for large companies
items.explanation: Like one lobby directory listing what is on each floor, the tree gives a single, predictable map starting at the root, so you know where things live instead of guessing.

items.prompt: What does `cd /home/alice` do?
choices: Copies alice's files to the root
Moves you into the /home/alice folder so it becomes your current location
items.explanation: `cd` means "change directory": it moves your current location to the folder you name. To see what is inside afterward you would run `ls`.

lessons.title: The Standard Map: Where Things Live
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now that everything is one tree, you need to know which top-level folder holds what. Happily, Linux follows a *standard map* (its official name is the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard, or FHS) so the layout is the same across systems. Learn the map once and you will know where to look on any Linux machine.

**Build.** Here are the top-level folders you will meet most often:

- `/home` -- your personal files. Each user gets a folder here, like `/home/alice`.
- `/etc` -- system **configuration**: plain-text settings files for the whole machine. (Say it "et-see".)
- `/bin` and `/usr/bin` -- **programs** (the actual command files like `ls` and `cp`). "bin" is short for *binaries*, meaning runnable programs.
- `/usr` -- the bulk of installed software lives under here.
- `/var` -- data that **changes** over time, especially logs in `/var/log` (a diary of what the system has been doing).
- `/tmp` -- a scratch space for temporary files; usually wiped when the machine restarts.
- `/dev` -- your hardware **devices**, presented as files (more on that next lesson).
- `/boot` -- the files needed to start the computer.
- `/proc` -- not real files on a disk, but a live window into what the system is doing right now.

```bash
ls /etc       # peek at system config files
ls /var/log   # the system's log files (its diary)
which ls      # shows the program file behind a command, e.g. /usr/bin/ls
```

**Recap.** Config goes in `/etc`, your stuff in `/home`, programs in `/bin` and `/usr/bin`, logs in `/var/log`. When something breaks, the map tells you where to look.

items.prompt: Using the map above, where would you look for the system's log files?
items.explanation: Logs are data that keeps changing as the system runs, so they live under /var (variable data), specifically /var/log. /etc holds configuration, not logs.

items.prompt: Which top-level folder holds system-wide configuration (settings) files?
items.explanation: /etc is the home of system configuration: plain-text settings files for the machine. /bin holds programs, /var holds changing data like logs, and /tmp is scratch space.

items.prompt: Where do an ordinary user's personal files live?
choices: In /home, in a folder named after them, like /home/alice
items.explanation: Each user gets a personal folder under /home, such as /home/alice. /etc is for system config, /bin for programs, and /proc is a live view of the running system.

items.prompt: The word "bin" in /bin and /usr/bin is short for what, and what do those folders hold?
choices: "bin" = binaries (runnable programs); they hold the actual command files like ls and cp
items.explanation: "bin" stands for binaries, meaning runnable program files. When you type ls, the system runs the program file found in a bin folder such as /usr/bin/ls.

items.prompt: What is special about files placed in /tmp?
choices: They are scratch/temporary files and are usually cleared when the machine restarts
They can only be read by the root user
They are backed up every night
items.explanation: /tmp is a temporary scratch area; its contents are typically wiped on reboot. Do not keep anything important there. Long-lived data belongs in /home or /var.

items.prompt: Which folder holds the files the computer needs in order to start up?
items.explanation: /boot contains the startup files, including the Linux kernel image and the bootloader's files, that get the machine going before the rest of the tree is available.

items.prompt: You run `which ls` and it prints `/usr/bin/ls`. What did that tell you?
choices: The actual program file behind the ls command lives at /usr/bin/ls
That you have no permission to run ls
items.explanation: `which` reports the location of the program file that runs when you type a command. Here the ls command is the program file /usr/bin/ls, confirming programs live in bin folders.

items.prompt: A service is misbehaving. Based on the standard map, where do you look FIRST to see what it has been doing?
choices: /var/log, where the system keeps its logs
items.explanation: Logs are the system's diary of events and errors, and they live in /var/log. Knowing the map turns "where do I even look?" into a quick, confident answer.

lessons.title: Everything Is a File
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Here is one of Linux's most surprising ideas: almost everything looks like a file, even your hardware. Your keyboard, your disk, even a stream of random numbers, all show up as file-like things you can open and read.

**Build.** A normal file is just a named bundle of data. Linux extends that idea: devices appear as special files under `/dev`. For example `/dev/null` is a "trash can" file that throws away anything written to it, and `/dev/zero` hands out endless zeros. The payoff is that the same simple tools, opening, reading, and writing, work on a text file, a disk, or a device. You do not need a different toolset for each.

Underneath a filename, Linux stores the real file as an **inode** (think: index node) -- a small record holding the file's details: who owns it, its permissions, its size, timestamps, and where the data sits. Interestingly, the *name* is not stored in the inode. A folder is really just a list that pairs names with inodes.

Because names and inodes are separate, you can give one file a second name. A **hard link** is a second name pointing at the very same inode. A **symbolic link** (or *symlink*) is different: it is a tiny signpost file that just stores a path to another file by name; if you delete the target, the signpost dangles and points at nothing.

```bash
ls -l /dev/null        # a device shown as a file
echo hi > note.txt
ln -s note.txt link    # symlink: a signpost pointing at note.txt
ls -l link             # shows  link -> note.txt
```

**Recap.** "Everything is a file" means devices use the same read/write tools as text. Names live in folders; the real file is an inode. A hard link is a second name; a symlink is a signpost storing a path.

items.prompt: What does the phrase "everything is a file" practically mean in Linux?
choices: Every file is the same size
Even devices like disks and keyboards can be accessed with the same open/read/write tools as ordinary files
You can only store text, never images
All files must live in one folder
items.explanation: Devices, and other resources, appear as file-like objects (mostly under /dev), so the same simple tools work everywhere. That uniformity is the whole point; it is not about file sizes.

items.prompt: Which folder is where hardware devices show up as special files?
items.explanation: /dev (short for devices) is where Linux presents hardware as files. /etc holds config, /var holds changing data, and /home holds personal files.

items.prompt: What is `/dev/null` commonly used for?
choices: A trash can: anything written to it is silently discarded
items.explanation: /dev/null is a special device file that throws away everything written to it, handy when you want to discard output you do not care about. It does not store anything.

items.prompt: What is an inode?
choices: A small record holding a file's details (owner, permissions, size, where the data lives) -- but not its name
The folder a file is stored in
items.explanation: An inode is the behind-the-scenes record of a file's metadata and where its data sits. Notably, the filename is NOT in the inode; names are kept in folders that map a name to an inode.

items.prompt: If a filename is not stored in the inode, where is it kept?
choices: In a folder, which is really a list pairing names with inodes
items.explanation: A folder (directory) is essentially a table that pairs each name with an inode number. That separation of name from inode is exactly what makes links possible.

items.prompt: What is a hard link?
choices: A copy of the file's data in a new location
A second name that points at the very same inode as the original
A shortcut that stores the path to another file
A link that only root can follow
items.explanation: A hard link is just another name for the same inode, so both names refer to one identical file. (A symlink, by contrast, only stores a path to a name.)

items.prompt: You run `ln -s note.txt link`, then delete note.txt. What is the state of `link`?
choices: link still holds the original data
link is a dangling symlink: a signpost now pointing at a path that no longer exists
link is automatically deleted as well
link turns into a hard link
items.explanation: A symlink only stores the path "note.txt" by name. Delete the target and the signpost dangles, pointing at nothing. A hard link would have kept the data, since it shares the inode.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a symbolic link and a hard link?
choices: A symlink stores a path to another name; a hard link is a second name for the same inode
There is no difference; they are two words for the same thing
A hard link can only point to folders
A symlink uses twice the disk space of the original file
items.explanation: A symlink is a signpost holding a path (so it can break if the target moves). A hard link shares the actual inode, so it is a true equal second name for the same file data.

lessons.title: Owners and Permissions: rwx for user, group, other
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Picture a shared document with sharing settings: some people can view, some can edit, others have no access. Linux gives every file the same kind of settings, and this is the foundation of Linux security.

**Build.** Every file has an **owner** (a user) and a **group** (a named set of users). Permissions are then set for three audiences: the **user** (owner), the **group**, and **other** (everyone else). Each audience gets three switches:

- **r** = read (look at the contents)
- **w** = write (change the contents)
- **x** = execute (run it, if it is a program)

Run `ls -l` and you will see a string like `-rwxr-xr--`. The first character is the type (`-` for a file, `d` for a directory). The next nine are three groups of three: owner, then group, then other. So `-rwxr-xr--` means: owner can read/write/run, group can read and run, everyone else can only read.

One classic gotcha: on a **folder**, `x` does not mean "run". It means "may enter / pass through this folder". Without `x` on a folder you cannot `cd` into it or reach anything inside, even if you can see its name.

```bash
ls -l file.txt        # shows the permission string and owner
chmod u+x script.sh   # give the owner permission to run it
chmod g-w file.txt    # take write away from the group
chown alice file.txt  # make alice the owner (admin only)
```

`chmod` (change mode) edits the r/w/x switches; `chown` (change owner) sets who owns the file.

**Recap.** Owner, group, other each get read/write/execute. Read the string left to right in groups of three. On a folder, `x` means "enter it", not "run it".

items.prompt: Decode the permission string `-rwxr-xr--`. Which statement is TRUE?
choices: It is a folder; the owner can only read it
It is a file: the owner can read/write/run, the group can read and run, and everyone else can only read
It is a file that everyone can edit
It is a file; the owner has no permissions at all
items.explanation: First char `-` means a regular file. Then in threes: owner `rwx` (read+write+run), group `r-x` (read+run, a dash means that switch is off), other `r--` (read only). Read it left to right in groups of three.

items.prompt: Permissions are set for three audiences. What are they?
choices: User (the owner), group, and other (everyone else)
items.explanation: The three audiences are the owning user, the file's group, and other (everyone else). Read/write/execute are the three permissions each audience can be granted, not the audiences themselves.

items.prompt: On an ordinary file, what do r, w, and x mean?
choices: read (view contents), write (change contents), execute (run it as a program)
items.explanation: r = read the contents, w = write/change the contents, x = execute (run) the file as a program. A dash in place of any letter means that permission is switched off.

items.prompt: In an `ls -l` listing, the permission string begins with `d`, as in `drwxr-xr-x`. What does that leading `d` tell you?
choices: The item is a directory (folder), not a regular file
items.explanation: The first character is the type: `-` for a regular file and `d` for a directory. The nine characters after it are the owner/group/other permissions.

items.prompt: What does the execute (x) permission mean on a FOLDER, as opposed to a file?
choices: It lets you run the folder as a program
It lets you enter (traverse into) the folder and reach things inside it
It lets you rename the folder
It has no effect on folders
items.explanation: This is the classic gotcha: on a folder, x means "may enter / pass through". Without x you cannot cd into the folder or reach files inside, even if you can read its listing.

items.prompt: Which command changes a file's read/write/execute permissions?
items.explanation: chmod ("change mode") edits the r/w/x switches. chown changes who owns the file, chgrp changes the group, and ls -l only displays the permissions.

items.prompt: What does `chown alice file.txt` do?
choices: Makes alice the owner of file.txt
Deletes file.txt if alice owns it
items.explanation: chown ("change owner") sets who owns the file, here making alice the owner. Handing a file to another user normally requires administrator (root) privileges.

items.prompt: You wrote a program file and want only its owner to be allowed to run it. Which command adds that permission?
items.explanation: In symbolic form, u = user (owner), g = group, o = other, a = all. `u+x` adds execute for the owner only. `o+x` would add it for everyone else, and `a-x` would remove it from all.

items.prompt: Why are owners and permissions described as the foundation of Linux security?
choices: They control who can read, change, or run each file, keeping users from interfering with each other's and the system's files
They encrypt every file by default
They hide files from the owner
items.explanation: Permissions decide who may read, write, or run a file. That is how Linux keeps one user out of another's files and protects system files, the everyday basis of its security.

lessons.title: Number Shortcuts and Special Bits
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Typing `chmod u+x,g+r,o+r` gets tedious. Linux offers a shorthand: a three-digit number that captures all nine permission switches at once. You will see numbers like `755` everywhere, so it helps to read them.

**Build.** The trick is to give each switch a value: **r = 4, w = 2, x = 1**. For one audience, add up the switches that are on. So `rwx` = 4+2+1 = 7, `r-x` = 4+0+1 = 5, `r--` = 4. Do this for owner, group, and other and you get three digits.

That is why `rwxr-xr-x` is **755**: owner 7, group 5, other 5. A common file setting is **644** (`rw-r--r--`): owner can read and write, everyone else read only. You apply it with `chmod 755 file` or `chmod 644 file`. This is just another way to write the same thing as the symbolic `u+x` style from the last lesson.

```bash
chmod 755 script.sh   # rwxr-xr-x  (owner full; others read+run)
chmod 644 notes.txt   # rw-r--r--  (owner read/write; others read)
```

**Special bits (light touch).** A few folders use extra bits beyond rwx. The most common is the **sticky bit** on `/tmp`: because everyone can write there, the sticky bit adds a rule that you may only delete *your own* files, not other people's. There are also **setuid** and **setgid** bits that let a program run with its owner's or group's powers; you will see these occasionally but rarely set them yourself as a beginner.

**Recap.** r=4, w=2, x=1; add per audience to get a digit; three digits set everything. 755 = rwxr-xr-x, 644 = rw-r--r--. Special bits like the sticky bit on /tmp add a few extra safety rules.

items.prompt: Using r=4, w=2, x=1, what three-digit number equals `rwxr-xr-x`?
items.explanation: Owner rwx = 4+2+1 = 7; group r-x = 4+0+1 = 5; other r-x = 4+0+1 = 5. So rwxr-xr-x is 755. 777 would be rwx for everyone; 644 is rw-r--r--.

items.prompt: In the number shortcut, what value does each permission have?
items.explanation: read = 4, write = 2, execute = 1. You add up the ones that are on for each audience. These specific values let every combination map to a unique digit from 0 to 7.

items.prompt: For one audience, the digit 7 means which permissions are on?
choices: Read and write, but not execute
items.explanation: 7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = read + write + execute, so all three switches are on. That is the rwx triad fully enabled.

items.prompt: What does `chmod 644 notes.txt` set the permissions to?
choices: rw-r--r--  (owner read/write; group and other read only)
items.explanation: Owner 6 = 4+2 = rw-; group 4 = r--; other 4 = r--. So 644 is rw-r--r--: the owner can read and write, everyone else can only read. A very common setting for documents.

items.prompt: What single digit represents the permissions `r-x`?
items.explanation: r = 4 and x = 1, and w is off (0), so 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. (6 would be rw-, and 7 would be rwx.)

items.prompt: How does the number form (like 755) relate to the symbolic form (like u+x) from the previous lesson?
choices: They are two ways of expressing the same read/write/execute permissions
The number form only works for folders
The symbolic form is more powerful and the number form is fake
items.explanation: Both set the same r/w/x switches. Numbers set all nine at once to an exact state; symbolic mode (u+x, g-w) nudges individual switches up or down. Use whichever is clearer for the task.

items.prompt: Why does the shared /tmp folder use the sticky bit?
choices: So that even though everyone can write there, you may only delete your own files, not other people's
items.explanation: /tmp is writable by everyone, which would normally let anyone delete anyone's files. The sticky bit adds the rule that only a file's owner (or root) can delete it, protecting people's scratch files from each other.

items.prompt: At a beginner level, what is the rough idea of the setuid bit on a program?
choices: It lets the program run with the powers of the program's owner, rather than the person running it
It deletes the program after one use
items.explanation: setuid lets a program run with its owner's privileges, so an ordinary user can perform a tightly controlled task that normally needs more power. You will see it occasionally; you rarely need to set it yourself early on.

items.prompt: Someone runs `chmod 777 secret.txt`. Why is that usually a bad idea?
choices: 777 is not a valid permission number
777 gives read, write, AND execute to everyone, so any user on the system can change the file
items.explanation: 777 = rwx for owner, group, and other, meaning everyone can read, modify, and run it. That is wide open and rarely what you want for a file with private contents.

lessons.title: Beyond the Basics: ACLs and Capabilities
lessons.content:
**Hook.** The owner/group/other model is simple, but sometimes too simple. What if you want to share one file with exactly one extra person, who is not the owner and not in the group? For situations like that, Linux has a few extra tools. You will not need these every day, but it helps to know they exist.

**Build.** First, **ACLs** (Access Control Lists). The plain model names only one owner and one group. An ACL lets you add extra named people or groups with their own permissions, without disturbing the owner or group. You add an entry with `setfacl` and view them with `getfacl`. A clue that a file has ACLs: in `ls -l` you will see a small `+` at the end of its permission string.

```bash
setfacl -m u:bob:rw report.txt   # also let bob read and write
getfacl report.txt               # list all the extra permission entries
```

Second, **capabilities**. Traditionally, a program that needed one special power had to run as the all-powerful root user, which is risky: if it is tricked, an attacker gets *everything*. Capabilities split root's power into many small, separate powers, so a program can be granted just the one it needs (for example, the ability to send a network ping) and nothing more. This is the modern, safer alternative to giving a program full root powers. You inspect them with `getcap` and set them with `setcap`.

```bash
getcap /usr/bin/ping   # see which single power ping was granted
```

**Recap.** ACLs add extra named users/groups beyond owner/group/other (look for the `+` in `ls -l`). Capabilities hand a program one specific power instead of all of root, which is safer. Both are tools to reach for only when the basic model is not enough.

items.prompt: What can an ACL do that the basic owner/group/other model cannot?
choices: Grant permissions to extra named users or groups, beyond the single owner and group
Hide the file from the owner
items.explanation: The basic model names only one owner and one group. An ACL lets you add more named users or groups with their own permissions, perfect for sharing with one extra person without changing ownership.

items.prompt: In `ls -l`, a file's permission string ends with a `+`, like `-rw-r--r--+`. What does that signal?
choices: An ACL is present, granting extra permissions beyond the basic ones shown
The owner is the root user
items.explanation: A trailing `+` means the file has an ACL with extra entries beyond the standard nine bits. Use getfacl to see the full set of who-can-do-what.

items.prompt: You must give exactly one extra user, bob, read and write on report.txt, without changing its owner or group. Which command fits?
items.explanation: setfacl -m u:bob:rw adds an ACL entry for bob alone. chmod o+rw would open the file to everyone, chown would hand the whole file to bob, and 777 opens it to all.

items.prompt: Which command shows all the ACL entries on a file?
items.explanation: getfacl lists every ACL entry (the owner/group/other lines plus any extra named users or groups). getcap is for capabilities, and chmod changes basic permissions.

items.prompt: What problem do capabilities solve?
choices: They split root's all-or-nothing power into small pieces, so a program can get just the one power it needs
items.explanation: Instead of running a program as full root (which is risky), capabilities grant just one specific power. If the program is compromised, the damage is limited to that single power.

items.prompt: Why is granting a program one capability safer than running it as full root?
choices: Because if the program is tricked, an attacker gets only that one narrow power, not control of the whole system
items.explanation: Full root means full control of the machine, so a compromised root program is a disaster. A single capability limits the blast radius to just that one ability.

items.prompt: Which command lets you inspect the capabilities granted to a program like /usr/bin/ping?
items.explanation: getcap reads a program's capabilities. getfacl reads ACLs, chmod changes basic permissions, and ls -l shows the rwx bits but not the capabilities.

items.prompt: As a beginner, when should you reach for ACLs and capabilities?
choices: Only when the basic owner/group/other model and ordinary permissions are not enough for the job
Only on the root user's files
items.explanation: The basic model handles most needs. ACLs and capabilities are extra tools for the less common cases (sharing with one extra person, or granting one narrow power) and are good to know exist.
